en.extract:
The Australian owlet-nightjar (Aegotheles cristatus) is a nocturnal bird found in open woodland across Australia and in southern New Guinea. It is the smallest nocturnal bird found in Australia, making it prone to predation. It is colloquially known as the moth owl or fairy owl and Jarlajirrpi by the Warlpiri people. It is the most common nocturnal bird in Australia. Despite suffering from predation and competition by introduced species, it is not considered threatened.


Taxonomy
The Australian owlet-nightjar was formally described as Caprimulgus cristatus in 1790 by the English naturalist George Shaw based on a specimen collected in New South Wales, Australia. The species was described in the same year by John Latham as Caprimulgus novaehollandiae but Shaw's account is believed to be earlier and thus has precedence. The Australian owlet-nightjar was previously placed in Caprimulgiformes but updated taxonomy now places it in Aegotheliformes. The Australian owlet-nightjar is now one of ten species placed in the genus Aegotheles that was introduced in 1827 by the naturalists Nicholas Vigors and Thomas Horsfield. It is the type species of the genus. The specific epithet cristatus is Latin meaning "crested", "plumed" or "tufted".
Two subspecies are recognised:

A. c. cristatus (Shaw, 1790) – Trans-Fly (central south New Guinea), Port Moresby area (southeast New Guinea) and Australia
A. c. tasmanicus Mathews, 1918 – Tasmania (southeast Australia)


Description

The Australian owlet-nightjar is It is 21–25 cm long, making it a small to medium-sized owlet-nightjar with grey upperparts and a white, barred front and a distinct dark and pale patterning on the head. It has large dark eyes that are non-reflective when a light is shone upon them; unlike many other nocturnal birds.  In Northern Australia, females can also have a rufous morph. It is possible that males also have a rufous morph in this area, but it is far less distinct in comparison to the female's. This rufous morph is only in the North of Australia and is not found in coastal South Eastern Australia. Hatchlings are covered in dense white down which is replaced by grey down after about 10 days, this grey down is then replaced with juvenile plumage. Juvenile birds are similar to adults with slightly more spotting, shorter face bristles, and blurred markings on the face and nape. The plumage is overall paler in desert populations.
Australian owlet-nightjars are adapted to live in open woodland, with more pointed wings and larger feet, unlike most of the rest of the family that live in dense forest (though some individuals of this species can and do live in such habitat in Queensland and New Guinea).


Physiology


Thermoregulation
Australian owlet-nightjars have an overall higher body temperature in summer and lower body temperature in winter. To combat lower temperatures in winter, Australian owlet-nightjars increase feather insulation to keep warm. During summer, Australian owlet-nightjars build up fat deposits, of which they have fully used up by mid-winter. Australian owlet-nightjars are nocturnal hunters, and in winter they have the advantage of longer periods of darkness for hunting, despite struggling with lower abundance of arthropods to eat. While resting during the day, Australian owlet-nightjars select roosts that are warmed by the sun to lower the energy required to keep them warm.
In hot weather, birds use evaporative cooling to cool their body temperature. This relies on panting and gular fluttering. Being a nocturnal species, Australian owlet-nightjars are reluctant to leave roost sites during the day, and cannot seek out water to rehydrate that lost from panting. In order to conserve water and avoid leaving their roost, Australian owlet-nightjars have an efficient thermoregulation system to prevent excessive moisture loss and dehydration, making them highly heat tolerant. Australian owlet-nightjars roost in hollows which protects them from full exposure to the sun and heat during the day, this is possibly why they are comparatively less heat-tolerant than related species that roost out in the open on arid ground.


Torpor
Australian owlet-nightjars are well known for frequently entering torpor, and during winter in Australia this often happens daily.  They usually enter torpor once a day around dawn for 3–4 hours, if they enter torpor twice in a day it is always a morning bout followed by a bout in the afternoon or night. Australian owlet-nightjars prefer roosting in tree hollows, but will occasionally roost in rock cavities, which are warmer and more temperature stable. In one study, Australian owlet-nightjars that roosted in rocks entered torpor half as often as those roosting in trees, and if they entered a second torpor it was more likely to be in the afternoon, while those in tree crevices were more likely to enter a second torpor at night. Australian owlet-nightjars tend to enter torpor when the atmospheric temperature is below 6.8 °C but may even do so at temperatures as high as 14.5 °C.
Torpor can be used to protect against fluctuating temperatures or food shortages by reducing the body's metabolism, heart rate, and temperature. Australian owlet-nightjars enter torpor more during periods of low arthropod abundance, likely to conserve energy and reduce the need for food. Australian owlet-nightjars do not enter torpor during the breeding season due to warm temperatures and an abundance of arthropods.


Habitat and distribution
Australian-owlet nightjars are widespread in Australia and are a hollow-dependent species, meaning they use tree hollows for both roosting and nesting. In Australia they can be found in suburban and urban-fringe environments. Rivers and waterways are important to this species; a study in Australia found that they were more likely to be found in areas with waterways than those without.
They are found in a variety of habitats such as open woodland, mallee scrub, tropical woodlands, and occasionally mangroves, open grasslands, and rainforests. In New Guinea this species is commonly found in savannah, while in Australia it has a preference for open mature woodland and habitats containing eucalyptus and acacia.


Behaviour


Vocalizations
The Australian owlet-nightjar is frequently heard but not often seen. Adult Australian owlet-nightjars appear to have a few main calls described as: "churr, cry, yuk, cho-ok, hiss, screech and other short harsh rattling notes".


Roosting

Australian owlet-nightjars prefer roosting in tree hollows that are closer to the ground and in areas of trees with lots of other cavities. On average they move between roost sites over a distance of about 300m every 9 days and in one study used between 2-6 different cavities over a 6 month period. The same study found that Australian owlet-nightjars tended to roost alone and favoured Stringybark and Manna Gum. They were also found in yellow box, red gum, apple box, and two dead Eucalyptus trees that could not be identified to species. Cavity choice is essential as Australian owlet-nightjars spend more than half their time roosting. It is still unknown why Australian owlet-nightjars use hollows to roost as many related species roost in the open on tree limbs or on the ground. Some hypotheses are predation and ectoparasite avoidance based on similar behaviour in bats, but further research is needed.
Normally Australian owlet-nightjars roost in tree hollows and snags but there are records of them roosting in man-made structures like forestry boom gates in Tasmania. These gates are used to restrict access to private and crown land and when opened occasionally flush Australian owlet-nightjars that were roosting inside. They have also been found roosting in roof cavities and chimneys.


Threat display
Australian owlet-nightjars are often quiet and passive when disturbed. Many accounts of them being encountered in nest boxes report that they sit quietly on their eggs or young, occasionally flushing if they sense approaching danger. There are a few anecdotal reports of Australian owlet-nightjars flattening themselves or puffing up to seem larger, swaying side to side, opening their mouths wide to show their pink gape and hissing. Both young and older birds have been reported to do this when disturbed while roosting or nesting.


Hunting and diet
Australian owlet-nightjars are sally type foragers and rarely forage from the ground. They hunt almost exclusively at night, perhaps as a way to mitigate predation. They use their large eyes, good hearing, and rictal bristles to locate and capture prey. The Australian owlet-nightjar feeds at night by diving from perches and snatching insects from the air, ground or off trunks and branches, in the manner of a flycatcher. It may also feed on the wing. It feeds on most insects, particularly beetles, grasshoppers and ants.

Australian owlet-nightjars are considered 'lunarphilic' which means they prefer hunting on nights with moonlight and are crepuscular (hunting at dawn and dusk). Australian owlet-nightjars that hunt on moonlit nights face a trade-off, as they hunt using vision but are also at a greater risk from predation by other visual hunters (e.g. owls). Australian owlet-nightjars are relatively small and so face higher rates of predation than related species, this is likely why their hunting behaviour is reduced on nights with full moons, when they only hunt enough to meet their energy requirements.
The species is itself prey to another nocturnal carnivore, Macroderma gigas, a larger microchiropteran known as the ghost bat.


Reproduction
The Australian owlet-nightjar nests mainly in holes in trees (or in other holes and crevices), which is provisioned with leaves by both of the pair. It is thought that the frequent addition of eucalyptus leaves is because they act as a beneficial insecticide. Australian owlet-nightjar pairs are thought to mate for life, and pairs breed between August and December. Three or four eggs are laid, and incubated by the female for just under a month. Both the adults feed the chicks, which fledge after a month. The young birds are reported to stay close to the parents for several months after they fledge.


Relationship with humans
Australian owlet-nightjars have an important cultural significance to the Warlpiri people; it is thought of as connected to the Kurdaitcha man, a ritual executioner. The Australian owlet-nightjars is called Jarlajirrpi and its call is said to warn of the approach of the Kurdaitcha.
People also set up nesting boxes for Australian owlet-nightjars.


Status and management
Australian owlet-nightjars are adaptable and are considered Least Concern. They are threatened by logging and clearcutting for development and timber which reduces their nesting and roosting habitat. They also face predation risk from introduced predators like foxes and cats. Forest and bush fires have increased in intensity and frequency due to climate change and pose a significant risk to roosting and nesting trees. Being primarily insectivores, pesticides pose a risk to their food source.


References


Further reading


External links
Link to recording of Australian owlet-nightjar https://xeno-canto.org/287370

The '''Australian owlet-nightjar''' ('''''Aegotheles cristatus''''') is a [[nocturnal]] [[bird]] found in open woodland across [[Australia]] and in southern [[New Guinea]]. It is the smallest nocturnal bird found in Australia, making it prone to predation.<ref name="birdsinbackyards.net">{{Cite web |title=Australian Owlet-nightjar {{!}} BIRDS in BACKYARDS |url=https://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Aegotheles-cristatus |access-date=2025-11-05 |website=www.birdsinbackyards.net}}</ref> It is colloquially known as the '''moth owl''' or '''fairy owl'''<ref name=":7">{{Cite web |title=June bird of the month: Australian Owlet-nightjar |url=https://birdlife.org.au/news/june-bird-of-the-month-australian-owlet-nightjar/ |access-date=2025-11-05 |website=BirdLife Australia |language=en}}</ref> and '''Jarlajirrpi''' by the [[Warlpiri people]]. It is the most common nocturnal bird in [[Australia]]. Despite suffering from predation and competition by [[introduced species]], it is not considered threatened.<ref name=IUCN/>

==Taxonomy==
The Australian owlet-nightjar was [[species description|formally described]] as ''Caprimulgus cristatus'' in 1790 by the English naturalist [[George Shaw (biologist)|George Shaw]] based on a specimen collected in [[New South Wales]], Australia.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Shaw | first=George | author-link=George Shaw (biologist) | editor-last=White | editor-first=John | editor-link=John White (surgeon) | date=1790 | title=Journal of a Voyage to New South Wales : with sixty-five plates of nondescript animals, birds, lizards, serpents, curious cones of trees and other natural productions | location=London | publisher=Printed for J. Debrett | page=241 Supp | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47182828 }} For Shaw as author of the specific name see: {{Cite journal | last=Sherborn | first=C. Davies | date=1891 | title=Note on the authors of the specific names in John White's 'Journal of a Voyage to New South Wales', 1790 | journal=The Annals and Magazine of Natural History, Including Zoology, Botany, and Geology | series=6th series | volume=7 | issue=42 | page=535 | doi=10.1080/00222939109460662 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/25165016 }}</ref> The species was described in the same year by [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] as ''Caprimulgus novaehollandiae'' but Shaw's account is believed to be earlier and thus has precedence.<ref>{{cite book| last=Latham | first=John | author-link=John Latham (ornithologist) | year=1790 | title=Index Ornithologicus, Sive Systema Ornithologiae: Complectens Avium Divisionem In Classes, Ordines, Genera, Species, Ipsarumque Varietates | volume=2 | language=Latin | location=London | publisher = Leigh & Sotheby | page=588 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/53510013 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Hemming | editor-first=Francis | year=1956 | title=Opinions and Declarations Rendered by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature | volume=1 Section D | location=London | publisher=International Trust for Zoological Nomenclature | chapter=Report by the Secretary on the relative dates of publication of the names ''Caprimulgus novaehollandiae'' Latham and ''Caprimulgus cristatus'' Shaw in White, both currently treated as having been published in 1790 | pages=204–206 | chapter-url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/34613429 }}</ref> The Australian owlet-nightjar was previously placed in [[Nightjar|Caprimulgiformes]] but updated [[taxonomy]] now places it in [[Owlet-nightjar|Aegotheliformes]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last=Fitzsimons |first=James A. |date=March 2024 |title=Hissing as part of threat display in the Australian Owlet-nightjar Aegotheles cristatus |journal=Ecology and Evolution |volume=14 |issue=3 |article-number=e11101 |doi=10.1002/ece3.11101 |issn=2045-7758 |pmc=10905222 |pmid=38435002 |bibcode=2024EcoEv..1411101F }}</ref> The Australian owlet-nightjar is now one of ten species placed in the genus ''[[Aegotheles]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by the naturalists [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Nicholas Vigors]] and [[Thomas Horsfield]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Owlet-nightjars, treeswifts, swifts | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/swifts/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=8 October 2025 }}</ref> It is the [[type species]] of the genus.<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=181 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476652 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''cristatus'' is [[Latin]] meaning "crested", "plumed" or "tufted".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=cristatus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=cristatus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=8 October 2025 }}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''A. c. cristatus'' ([[George Shaw (biologist)|Shaw]], 1790) – [[Trans-Fly savanna and grasslands|Trans-Fly]] (central south [[New Guinea]]), [[Port Moresby]] area (southeast [[New Guinea]]) and Australia
* ''A. c. tasmanicus'' [[Gregory Mathews|Mathews]], 1918 – [[Tasmania]] (southeast Australia)

==Description==
[[File:Aegotheles chrisoptus - Catlereigh Nature Reserve.jpg|left|thumb|Australian owlet-nightjar mainly nests in tree hollows.]]
The Australian owlet-nightjar is It is 21–25 cm long,<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Holyoak |first1=David T. |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=Australian Owlet-nightjar (Aegotheles cristatus), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/auonig1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.auonig1.01 |issn=2771-3105}}</ref> making it a small to medium-sized [[owlet-nightjar]] with [[grey]] upperparts and a [[white]], barred front and a distinct dark and pale patterning on the head. It has large dark eyes that are [[Reflection (physics)|non-reflective]] when a light is shone upon them; unlike many other nocturnal birds.<ref name="birdsinbackyards.net"/>  In Northern [[Australia (continent)|Australia]], females can also have a rufous morph.<ref name=":2" /> It is possible that males also have a rufous morph in this area, but it is far less distinct in comparison to the female's.<ref name=":2" /> This rufous morph is only in the North of Australia and is not found in coastal South Eastern Australia.<ref name=":2"/> [[Hatchling]]s are covered in dense white down which is replaced by grey down after about 10 days, this grey down is then replaced with juvenile plumage.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Holyoak |first=D.T. |title=Nightjars and their Allies |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2001 |isbn=0 19 854987 3 |location=United States |pages=224–231 |language=English}}</ref> Juvenile birds are similar to adults with slightly more spotting, shorter face bristles, and blurred markings on the face and [[nape]].<ref name=":2"/> The [[plumage]] is overall paler in [[desert]] populations.

Australian owlet-nightjars are adapted to live in open [[woodland]], with more pointed wings and larger feet, unlike most of the rest of the [[Family (biology)|family]] that live in dense [[forest]] (though some individuals of this species can and do live in such habitat in [[Queensland]] and [[New Guinea]]).

== Physiology ==

=== Thermoregulation ===
Australian owlet-nightjars have an overall higher [[Thermoregulation|body temperature]] in summer and lower body temperature in winter.<ref name="Doucette-2008">{{Cite journal |last1=Doucette |first1=Lisa I. |last2=Geiser |first2=Fritz |date=2008-12-01 |title=Seasonal variation in thermal energetics of the Australian owlet-nightjar (Aegotheles cristatus) |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S109564330801088X |journal=Comparative Biochemistry and Physiology Part A: Molecular & Integrative Physiology |volume=151 |issue=4 |pages=615–620 |doi=10.1016/j.cbpa.2008.07.025 |pmid=18721893 |issn=1095-6433}}</ref> To combat lower temperatures in winter, Australian owlet-nightjars increase feather [[Thermal insulation|insulation]] to keep warm.<ref name="Doucette-2008" /> During summer, Australian owlet-nightjars build up [[Adipose tissue|fat deposits]], of which they have fully used up by mid-winter.<ref name="Doucette-2008" /> Australian owlet-nightjars are nocturnal hunters, and in winter they have the advantage of longer periods of darkness for hunting, despite struggling with lower abundance of [[arthropod]]s to eat.<ref name="Doucette-2008" /> While resting during the day, Australian owlet-nightjars select roosts that are warmed by the sun to lower the energy required to keep them warm.<ref name="Doucette-2008" />

In hot weather, birds use [[Evaporation|evaporative cooling]] to cool their body temperature.<ref name="O'Connor-2018">{{Cite journal |last1=O'Connor |first1=Ryan S. |last2=Smit |first2=Ben |last3=Talbot |first3=William A. |last4=Gerson |first4=Alexander R. |last5=Brigham |first5=R. Mark |last6=Wolf |first6=Blair O. |last7=McKechnie |first7=Andrew E. |date=2018-01-01 |title=Avian thermoregulation in the heat: is evaporative cooling more economical in nocturnal birds? |url=https://journals.biologists.com/jeb/article/221/17/jeb181420/19595/Avian-thermoregulation-in-the-heat-is-evaporative |journal=Journal of Experimental Biology |language=en |volume=221 |issue=17 |article-number=jeb.181420 |doi=10.1242/jeb.181420 |doi-broken-date=5 November 2025 |issn=1477-9145 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250821055604/https://journals.biologists.com/jeb/article/221/17/jeb181420/19595/Avian-thermoregulation-in-the-heat-is-evaporative |archive-date=2025-08-21}}</ref> This relies on [[Thermoregulation|panting]] and [[Thermoregulation|gular fluttering]]. Being a nocturnal species, Australian owlet-nightjars are reluctant to leave roost sites during the day, and cannot seek out water to [[Dehydration|rehydrate]] that lost from panting.<ref name="O'Connor-2018" /> In order to conserve water and avoid leaving their roost, Australian owlet-nightjars have an efficient [[thermoregulation]] system to prevent excessive moisture loss and [[dehydration]], making them highly heat tolerant.<ref name="O'Connor-2018" /> Australian owlet-nightjars roost in hollows which protects them from full exposure to the sun and heat during the day, this is possibly why they are comparatively less heat-tolerant than related species that roost out in the open on [[Aridity|arid]] ground.<ref name="O'Connor-2018" />

=== Torpor ===
Australian owlet-nightjars are well known for frequently entering [[torpor]], and during winter in Australia this often happens daily.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Doucette |first=Lisa I. |last2=Brigham |first2=R. Mark |last3=Pavey |first3=Chris R. |last4=Geiser |first4=Fritz |date=2011-08-21 |title=Roost type influences torpor use by Australian owlet-nightjars |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-011-0835-7 |journal=Naturwissenschaften |language=en |volume=98 |issue=10 |pages=845 |doi=10.1007/s00114-011-0835-7 |issn=1432-1904}}</ref>  They usually enter torpor once a day around [[dawn]] for 3–4 hours, if they enter torpor twice in a day it is always a morning bout followed by a bout in the [[afternoon]] or [[night]].<ref name=":0" /> Australian owlet-nightjars prefer roosting in [[tree hollow]]s, but will occasionally roost in rock cavities, which are warmer and more temperature stable.<ref name=":0" /> In one study, Australian owlet-nightjars that roosted in rocks entered torpor half as often as those roosting in trees, and if they entered a second torpor it was more likely to be in the afternoon, while those in tree crevices were more likely to enter a second torpor at night.<ref name=":0" /> Australian owlet-nightjars tend to enter torpor when the [[atmospheric temperature]] is below 6.8 °C but may even do so at temperatures as high as 14.5 °C.<ref name=":0" />

Torpor can be used to protect against fluctuating temperatures or food shortages by reducing the body's [[metabolism]], heart rate, and temperature.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Doucette |first=Lisa I. |last2=Brigham |first2=R. Mark |last3=Pavey |first3=Chris R. |last4=Geiser |first4=Fritz |date=2012-06-01 |title=Prey availability affects daily torpor by free-ranging Australian owlet-nightjars (Aegotheles cristatus) |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s00442-011-2214-7 |journal=Oecologia |language=en |volume=169 |issue=2 |pages=361–372 |doi=10.1007/s00442-011-2214-7 |issn=1432-1939}}</ref> Australian owlet-nightjars enter torpor more during periods of low [[arthropod]] abundance, likely to conserve energy and reduce the need for food.<ref name=":1" /> Australian owlet-nightjars do not enter torpor during the [[Seasonal breeder|breeding season]] due to warm temperatures and an abundance of arthropods.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Brigham |first=R. Mark |last2=Körtner |first2=Gerhard |last3=Maddocks |first3=Tracy A. |last4=Geiser |first4=Fritz |date=2000 |title=Seasonal Use of Torpor by Free‐Ranging Australian Owlet‐Nightjars (Aegotheles cristatus) |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/317755 |journal=Physiological and Biochemical Zoology: Ecological and Evolutionary Approaches |volume=73 |issue=5 |pages=613–620 |doi=10.1086/317755 |issn=1522-2152}}</ref>

== Habitat and distribution ==
Australian-owlet nightjars are widespread in Australia and are a hollow-dependent species, meaning they use tree hollows for both roosting and nesting.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Weaving |first=M. J. |last2=White |first2=J. G. |last3=Isaac |first3=B. |last4=Cooke |first4=R. |date=2011-03-01 |title=The distribution of three nocturnal bird species across a suburban-forest gradient |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1071/MU09117 |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |language=EN |doi=10.1071/MU09117}}</ref> In Australia they can be found in [[suburb]]an and [[Peri-urbanisation|urban-fringe]] environments.<ref name=":3" /> [[River]]s and [[waterway]]s are important to this species; a study in Australia found that they were more likely to be found in areas with waterways than those without.<ref name=":3" />

They are found in a variety of [[Habitat (ecology)|habitats]] such as open woodland, [[Mallee Woodlands and Shrublands|mallee]] scrub, [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|tropical woodlands]], and occasionally [[mangrove]]s, open [[grassland]]s, and [[rainforest]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Holyoak |first=David T. |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=Australian Owlet-nightjar (Aegotheles cristatus), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/auonig1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.auonig1.01 |issn=2771-3105}}</ref> In [[New Guinea]] this species is commonly found in [[savanna]]h, while in Australia it has a preference for open mature woodland and habitats containing [[eucalyptus]] and [[acacia]].<ref name=":2" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Vocalizations ===
The Australian owlet-nightjar is frequently heard but not often seen. Adult Australian owlet-nightjars appear to have a few main calls described as: "churr, cry, yuk, cho-ok, hiss, screech and other short harsh rattling notes".<ref name=":4" />

=== Roosting ===
[[File:Australian Owlet-nightjar 5557.jpg|thumb|328x328px|Australian owlet-nightjar roosting in a man-made structure]]
Australian owlet-nightjars prefer roosting in tree hollows that are closer to the ground and in areas of trees with lots of other cavities.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Brigham |first=R. Mark |last2=Debus |first2=Stephen J. S. |last3=Geiser |first3=Fritz |date=1998 |title=Cavity selection for roosting, and roosting ecology of forest-dwelling Australian Owlet-nightjars (Aegotheles cristatus) |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1442-9993.1998.tb00750.x |journal=Australian Journal of Ecology |language=en |volume=23 |issue=5 |pages=424–429 |doi=10.1111/j.1442-9993.1998.tb00750.x |issn=1442-9993}}</ref> On average they move between roost sites over a distance of about 300m every 9 days and in one study used between 2-6 different cavities over a 6 month period.<ref name=":5" /> The same study found that Australian owlet-nightjars tended to roost alone and favoured [[Stringybark]] and [[Eucalyptus viminalis|Manna Gum]].<ref name=":5" /> They were also found in [[Eucalyptus melliodora|yellow box]], red gum, [[Eucalyptus bridgesiana|apple box]], and two dead [[Eucalyptus]] trees that could not be identified to species.<ref name=":5" /> Cavity choice is essential as Australian owlet-nightjars spend more than half their time roosting.<ref name=":5" /> It is still unknown why Australian owlet-nightjars use hollows to roost as many related species roost in the open on tree limbs or on the ground.<ref name=":5" /> Some hypotheses are predation and [[Parasitism|ectoparasite]] avoidance based on similar behaviour in bats, but further research is needed.<ref name=":5" />

Normally Australian owlet-nightjars roost in tree hollows and [[Snag (ecology)|snags]] but there are records of them roosting in [[Artificiality|man-made structures]] like forestry [[Bar gate|boom gates]] in [[Tasmania]].<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last=Wapstra |first=Mark |date=2020-08-20 |title=Two records of an Australian Owlet-nightjar 'Aegotheles cristatus' roosting in forestry boom-gates in Tasmanian forests |url=https://search.informit.org/doi/10.3316/INFORMIT.358273215578266 |journal=Australian Field Ornithology |volume=33 |pages=156–158 |doi=10.3316/informit.358273215578266}}</ref> These gates are used to restrict access to private and [[crown land]] and when opened occasionally flush Australian owlet-nightjars that were roosting inside. They have also been found roosting in [[Roof|roof cavities]] and [[chimney]]s.<ref name=":6" />

=== Threat display ===
Australian owlet-nightjars are often quiet and passive when disturbed.<ref name=":4" /> Many accounts of them being encountered in [[nest box]]es report that they sit quietly on their eggs or young, occasionally flushing if they sense approaching danger.<ref name=":4" /> There are a few [[Anecdotal evidence|anecdotal reports]] of Australian owlet-nightjars flattening themselves or puffing up to seem larger, swaying side to side, opening their mouths wide to show their pink [[Beak|gape]] and hissing.<ref name=":4" /> Both young and older birds have been reported to do this when disturbed while roosting or nesting.<ref name=":4" />

=== Hunting and diet ===
Australian owlet-nightjars are [[Hawking (birds)|sally type foragers]] and rarely forage from the ground.<ref name=":7" /> They hunt almost exclusively at night, perhaps as a way to mitigate predation. They use their large eyes, good hearing, and [[Beak|rictal bristles]] to locate and capture prey.<ref name=":7" /> The Australian owlet-nightjar feeds at night by diving from perches and snatching [[insect]]s from the air, ground or off trunks and branches, in the manner of a [[Old World flycatcher|flycatcher]]. It may also feed on the wing. It feeds on most insects, particularly [[beetle]]s, [[grasshopper]]s and [[ant]]s.
[[File:Australian Owlet-nightjar (Aegotheles cristatus) (8079579387).jpg|thumb|318x318px|Australian owlet-nightjar in a tree hollow]]
Australian owlet-nightjars are considered 'lunarphilic' which means they prefer hunting on nights with [[moonlight]] and are [[Crepuscular animal|crepuscular]] (hunting at dawn and dusk).<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal |last=Brigham |first=R. Mark |last2=Gutsell |first2=Robin C. A. |last3=Wiacek |first3=Richard S. |last4=Geiser |first4=Fritz |date=1999-12-01 |title=Foraging Behaviour in Relation to the Lunar Cycle by Australian Owlet-nightjars Aegotheles cristatus |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1071/MU99031 |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |language=EN |doi=10.1071/MU99031}}</ref> Australian owlet-nightjars that hunt on moonlit nights face a [[trade-off]], as they hunt using vision but are also at a greater risk from predation by other visual hunters (e.g. [[owl]]s).<ref name=":8" /> Australian owlet-nightjars are relatively small and so face higher rates of predation than related species, this is likely why their hunting behaviour is reduced on nights with full moons, when they only hunt enough to meet their [[Energy homeostasis|energy requirements]].<ref name=":8" />

The species is itself prey to another nocturnal carnivore, ''[[Macroderma gigas]]'', a larger [[microchiropteran]] known as the ghost bat.<ref name="Boles1999">{{cite journal |last1=Boles |first1=W. |date=June 1999 |title=Avian prey of the Australian Ghost Bat ''Macroderma gigas'' (Microchiroptera: Megadermatidae): prey characteristics and damage from predation |journal=Australian Zoologist |volume=31 |issue=1 |pages=82–91 |doi=10.7882/AZ.1999.009 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
The Australian owlet-nightjar nests mainly in holes in trees (or in other holes and crevices), which is provisioned with leaves by both of the pair. It is thought that the frequent addition of [[eucalyptus]] leaves is because they act as a beneficial [[insecticide]]. Australian owlet-nightjar pairs are thought to mate for life,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Doucette |first=Lisa I. |date=2010-07-01 |title=Home range and territoriality of Australian owlet-nightjars Aegotheles cristatus in diverse habitats |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10336-010-0497-9 |journal=Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=151 |issue=3 |pages=673–685 |doi=10.1007/s10336-010-0497-9 |issn=2193-7206}}</ref> and pairs breed between August and December<ref name=":9">{{Cite web |title=Australian Owlet-nightjar (Aegotheles cristatus) - Australian AVES |url=https://aussieanimals.com/profiles/australian-owletnightjar-aegotheles-cristatus/ |access-date=2025-11-06 |website=Aussie Animals |language=en-US}}</ref>'''.''' Three or four eggs are laid, and incubated by the female for just under a month. Both the adults feed the chicks, which [[fledge]] after a month. The young birds are reported to stay close to the parents for several months after they fledge.<ref name="HBW">{{cite book |last=Holyoak |first=D.T. |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=1999 |isbn=978-84-87334-25-2 |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=J. |volume=5: Barn-owls to Hummingbirds |location=Barcelona, Spain |pages=252–265 [264] |chapter=Family Aegothelidae (Owlet-nightjars) |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=A. |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=J. |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0005unse/page/264/mode/1up |chapter-url-access=registration}}</ref>

== Relationship with humans ==
Australian owlet-nightjars have an important cultural significance to the [[Warlpiri people]]; it is thought of as connected to the [[Kurdaitcha|Kurdaitcha man]], a ritual executioner.<ref name=":10">{{Cite journal |last=Gosford |first=Bob |title=Jarlajirrpi – the Australian Owlet-Nightjar and the ethnoornithology of the crepuscular assassin |url=https://www.academia.edu/11157443/Jarlajirrpi_the_Australian_Owlet_Nightjar_and_the_ethnoornithology_of_the_crepuscular_assassin}}</ref> The Australian owlet-nightjars is called ''Jarlajirrpi'' and its call is said to warn of the approach of the Kurdaitcha.<ref name=":10" />

People also set up nesting boxes for Australian owlet-nightjars.

== Status and management ==
Australian owlet-nightjars are adaptable and are considered [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]].<ref name=":9" /> They are threatened by [[logging]] and [[clearcutting]] for development and [[Lumber|timber]] which reduces their nesting and roosting habitat.<ref name=":9" /> They also face predation risk from [[Introduced species|introduced predators]] like [[fox]]es and [[cat]]s.<ref name=":9" /> [[Wildfire|Forest]] and [[Wildfire|bush fires]] have increased in intensity and frequency due to [[climate change]] and pose a significant risk to roosting and nesting trees.<ref name=":9" /> Being primarily [[insectivore]]s, [[pesticide]]s pose a risk to their food source.<ref name=":9" />
